macro_segments.title: The Status of the Fifty Shekel Fine (Knas) After Court Proceedings and Rabbi Shimon's Position on Its Identity

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Debate: Whether the Fifty Shekel Fine Retains Its Identity as Knas After Ha'amadah Be-Din
Rabbi Shimon's Exception: The Fifty Shekel Fine Remains Knas Despite Court Proceedings, Exempting from Korban Shavuah
Rava's Challenge: The Fine Becomes Mamon After Ha'amadah Be-Din Except by Korban Shavuah Due to Special Verses
Exceptional Scenario: When the Girl Matures (Bogeret) After Court, the Fine Becomes Mamon Yet Retains Exemption from Korban Shavuah
Resolution: Knas Retains Exemption Status If It Started as Knas, Even If Later Converted to Mamon

macro_segments.title: The Interpretive Dispute Between Rabbi Shimon and the Rabbanan on What the Father Implicitly Demands in a Rape Claim

micro_segments.title: The Underlying Question: Does the Father Implicitly Demand the Fixed Knas or the Unspecified Boshes and Pagam (Mamon)?
Rabbi Shimon's Rejoinder: Even If Court Proceedings Convert It to Mamon, the Torah (Ve-Kichesh) Exempts It from Korban
Rabbi Shimon's Rationale: A Person Won't Abandon the Fixed, Certain Amount for an Amorphous, Indeterminate One
The Rabbanan's Rationale: A Person Prefers What Cannot Be Escaped by Admission to What Leaves Room for Exemption

macro_segments.title: The Earnings and Property Rights of a Daughter Supported by the Estate After Her Father's Death

micro_segments.title: The Question Posed: Do Brothers Entitled to a Daughter's Earnings (Maaseh Yadayim) When the Estate Supports Her?
The Principle of Reciprocity: Unlike the Father's Unilateral Right, the Estate's Support Creates Questions About Earnings Entitlement
Widow Comparison: The Widow's Earnings Go to the Estate, But Does the Daughter's?
The Father's Different Concerns: Greater Priority for Daughter's Profit (Harvacha) vs. Widow's Dignity (Ziluta)

macro_segments.title: The Mishnah's Implication Regarding Daughter's Earnings After the Father's Death

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah Teaches: Earnings Incurred While Father Lived Transfer to Brothers, But Earnings After His Death Remain Hers
The Gemara's Challenge: Does This Imply She Keeps All Earnings or Only the Excess Beyond Support Costs?
Rava bar Ula's Proposal: The Daughter Keeps Only the Additional Earnings (Hadafah) Beyond the Cost of Her Support

macro_segments.title: Rav Yosef's Stronger Proof from the Packaging of Earnings and Found Objects in the Mishnah

micro_segments.title: The Problem with Found Objects: How Can One Collect a Found Object Before Acquiring It?
The Equation: Earnings Are Like Found Objects—Just as She Keeps Found Objects After Father's Death, She Keeps All Earnings
Why Found Objects Go to the Daughter: The Father's Volitional Support Creates an Aversion to Taking Everything She Finds
The Estate's Mandatory Obligation: Once the Father Dies, the Estate Must Support the Daughter, So She Keeps All Her Earnings and Found Objects

macro_segments.title: The Conclusive Ruling and Scriptural Proof That the Brothers Do Not Inherit the Father's Rights Over the Daughter's Earnings

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda in the Name of Rav: A Supported Daughter Keeps Her Earnings; The Brothers Do Not Inherit the Father's Rights
The Scriptural Basis: 'You Shall Bequeath Them to Your Sons, Not Your Daughters to Your Sons'—No Transfer of Paternal Rights
Rabba's Objection: Maybe the Verse Only Excludes Knas and Damages (Petzuim), Not Ordinary Earnings
The Distinction on Injuries: Non-Permanent vs. Permanent Injuries—Father Collects for the Former, Daughter for the Latter
Final Halacha: We Rule Like Rav—The Daughter Keeps Her Earnings; The Brothers Do Not Inherit Paternal Rights Over Earnings

macro_segments.title: The Ketubah Payment Rights When a Father Betrothes and Then Divorces or Widows His Daughter

micro_segments.title: Scenario 1 (Eirusin Only): If Divorced After Eirusin, the Father Collects the Ketubah Since the Daughter Remains Under His Control
Scenario 2 (Eirusin Only): If Widowed After Eirusin, the Father Collects the Ketubah for the Same Reason
Scenario 3 (Nissu'in): If Divorced or Widowed After Nissu'in, the Daughter Collects Both Ketubot Since She's No Longer Under Father's Control

macro_segments.title: The Ketubah Payment Rights When a Father Betrothes and Then Divorces or Widows His Daughter (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehuda's Position: Even After Nissu'in, If the Father Initiated Eirusin, He Retains Ketubah Rights from the First Marriage
The Gemara's Rejection: Once Nissu'in Occurs, the Father Loses All Rights Over the Daughter and Her Ketubah

macro_segments.title: The Mishnah's Choice of Two Deaths vs. Two Divorces as a Window Into the Halacha on Agunah and the Hazakah of Widowhood

micro_segments.title: Incidental Teaching: The Mishnah Avoids the Scenario of Two Deaths to Prevent Raising Questions About a Woman's Eligibility After Two Deaths
The Underlying Debate: If a Woman's Husbands Keep Dying, After How Many Deaths (Two or Three) Is She Presumed Unfit for Remarriage?
The Mishnah's Assumption: Following Rabbi, the Hazakah (Presumption) Takes Effect After Two Occurrences

topical_tags.term: Bat Hanizona min Ha'achim (daughter supported by brothers/estate)
Rav Shshet (opinion on daughters and earnings)
